title: Provisional migration estimates by citizenship now available | Stats NZ
content:
## Provisional migration estimates by citizenship now available
13 December 2018, 2:00pm
Provisional migration estimates are now available by broad country of citizenship, Stats NZ said today.
“This is the first time our customers are able to see the provisional migration estimates by country of citizenship," population insights senior manager Brooke Theyers said.
"Breaking down migration by citizenship gives customers insight into what drives changes in international flows into and out of New Zealand.”
The downloadable files below and the graphs in this release give estimates by three broad measures of country of citizenship – New Zealand, Australia, and ‘rest of the world’.
They are an indication of the migration statistics that will be available by more detailed country-of-citizenship data when our new outcomes-based approach is fully adopted in January 2019.
- Estimated monthly migrant arrivals by citizenship and country of last residence, 2013–18 – CSV Comma Separated Values, 52 KB

### Estimated monthly migrant arrivals by citizenship and country of last residence, 2013–18 – CSV
Comma Separated Values, 52 KB
- Estimated monthly migrant arrivals, migrant departures, and net migration, 2013–18 – CSV Comma Separated Values, 5 KB

### Estimated monthly migrant arrivals, migrant departures, and net migration, 2013–18 – CSV
Comma Separated Values, 5 KB
- Estimated monthly migrant arrivals, migrant departures, and net migration, by citizenship, 2013–18 – CSV Comma Separated Values, 27 KB

### Estimated monthly migrant arrivals, migrant departures, and net migration, by citizenship, 2013–18 – CSV
Comma Separated Values, 27 KB

### Recent net migration gains driven by non-New Zealand citizens
The migration estimates in the downloadable file ‘Estimated monthly migrant arrivals by citizenship and country of last residence, 2013–18 – CSV’ and figure 1 reinforce that the high net migration gains experienced in each of the years from 2015 to 2018 were driven by non-New Zealand (‘rest of world’) citizenship arrivals, complemented by fewer departures of New Zealand citizens.
Using outcomes data, New Zealand had net migration gains of between 60,000 and 70,000 non-New Zealand citizens in each of the years from 2015 to 2018. These compare with a net migration gain of 30,000 non-New Zealand citizens in 2012.
In contrast, the years from 2015 to 2018 showed net migration losses of several thousand New Zealand citizens each year. However, these were much smaller losses than the net outflow of 38,000 New Zealand citizens in 2012.
Overall, the recent estimated net flows based on outcomes are similar to those indicated by intentions (‘permanent and long-term’) data.
See Outcomes versus intentions: Measuring migration based on travel histories for background on the difference between outcomes and intentions.
Monthly migrant arrivals, migrant departures, and net migration, by citizenship, June 2013 to October 2018
Note: Error bars indicate a 95 percent probability interval.
See text alternative for figure 1, Monthly migrant arrivals, migrant departures, and net migration, by citizenship, June 2013 to October 2018.

### Migrant flows more accurately measured by outcomes data
We have been developing an outcomes-based approach to estimate migration into and out of New Zealand. Final estimates will be available 17 months after the reference month, but provisional estimates will be available after one month to maintain the timeliness of migration statistics (see Update on the development of provisional external migration statistics ).
The outcomes-based approach is a way of classifying border crossings as short-term or long-term, based on whether travellers actually spend 12 months (or more) of the following 16 months in New Zealand. By comparison, the intentions-based approach, which we used previously, relied on the stated plans of people when they arrived in or left New Zealand.
The new methodology is completely independent of passenger cards. It provides a sustainable ongoing method of classifying travellers now that passengers are no longer required to complete departure cards as they leave New Zealand.
The migration estimates in this release show that arrivals and departures are typically higher in the outcomes-based measure than the traditional intentions-based measure (see figure 2 for overview of patterns). However, net migration (arrivals minus departures) using outcomes has been lower and higher than intentions at different times (see also Outcomes versus intentions: Measuring migration based on travel histories ). In recent years, net migration using outcomes has been slightly lower than intentions.
Monthly migrant arrivals, migrant departures, and net migration, by measure, June 2013 to October 2018
Note: Error bars indicate a 95 percent probability interval.
See text alternative for figure 2, Monthly migrant arrivals, migrant departures, and net migration, by measure, June 2013 to October 2018.
After four months of data, the error bars become noticeably narrower (see figure 3). This shows that we can accurately classify most travellers as either short-term visitors or migrants after that time. Customers can have confidence in the migration estimates without having to wait 17 months for final estimates.
“It’s also interesting that seasonal migration flows are more accurately identified in the new data, such as migrant departures in November-December,” Mrs Theyers said.
“Some of these will be international students who were previously identified by departure cards as short-term visitors, because they made multiple trips to and from New Zealand during the course of their studies.”
Monthly net migration, by measure, November 2016 to October 2018
Note: Error bars indicate a 95 percent probability interval.
See text alternative for figure 3, Monthly net migration by measure, November 2016 to October 2018.

### New release schedule
Statistics previously published in International travel and migration and statistics on short-term movements previously published in International visitor arrivals to New Zealand will be published in a new release, International travel . Long-term movements will be published in the new International migration release. Both releases will be published on the same day, up to 30 working days after each reference month. November data, previously published just before Christmas, will now be published in January, and December data in February (see Release calendar ).
The new release schedule largely reflects the need to use integrated administrative data (ie Stats NZ’s Integrated Data Infrastructure ) to provide place-of-residence within New Zealand of migrants and short-term resident travellers. This replaces information from the departure card. The timing is also affected by the new method to produce provisional migration estimates.
For more information about the changes to migration statistics see Migration Data Transformation Project , which includes mock-ups of how the Excel summary tables will look in the new International travel and International migration releases from January 2019. Feedback on these new tables is welcome: email info@stats.govt.nz .

### Text alternative for figure 1
A time series graph with discrete data points showing migrant arrival, migrant departure, and net migration numbers for New Zealand and non-New Zealand citizens from June 2013 to October 2018. Data before July 2017 is final, while data from July 2017 onwards is provisional. Each provisional data point has 95 percent uncertainty bounds to show the level of confidence in the estimate. Data is available in downloadable file ‘Estimated monthly migrant arrivals, migrant departures, and net migration, by citizenship, 2013–18 – CSV'.

### Text alternative for figure 2
A time series graph with discrete data points showing migrant arrival, migrant departure, and net migration numbers for outcomes-based and intentions-based measures from June 2013 to October 2018. Data before July 2017 is final, while data from July 2017 onwards is provisional. Each provisional data point has 95 percent uncertainty bounds to show the level of confidence in the estimate. Outcomes data is available in downloadable file ‘Estimated monthly migrant arrivals, migrant departures, and net migration, 2013–18 –CSV’. Intentions data is available in Infoshare , subject category ‘Tourism’ and group ‘International Travel and Migration’.

### Text alternative for figure 3
A time series graph with discrete data points showing net migration numbers for outcomes-based and intentions-based measures from November 2016 to October 2018. Data before July 2017 is final, while data from July 2017 onwards is provisional. Each provisional data point has 95 percent uncertainty bounds to show the level of confidence in the estimate. Outcomes data is available in downloadable file ‘Estimated monthly migrant arrivals, migrant departures, and net migration, 2013–18 – CSV’.
Kim Dunstan 03 964 8330 info@stats.govt.nz
